item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's understandable. I put in the blog to simply show an example of what 3811's effects would do in the long term, and if I'm being honest, I coded Abi to become a fair bit annoying. Her whole thing is that she loses her inhibitions, so she doesn't give a fuck how people think about her unless she cares about them.

I hope you at least liked the rest of the skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The blog posts felt like filler and dragged on for far too long. I feel it is a thematic mismatch to go into so much detail about the circumstances of someone's life and less detail about the anomaly itself. Also, pardon me for saying this, but I honestly found Abi annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a few things standing in the way of making this article really shine, chief among them the agonizingly long interlude of the blogger's story. It intrudes on the main narrative without bringing anything fresh to the table. It features a basic, flat character magick'd on a collision course with fate - and tells in a roundabout manner what is already known (or conveyed more efficiently by being alluded to via interview).

It might have served an illustrative purpose if the mechanics of the anomaly weren't already plainly laid out, or it might have offered a readers a character to connect to if they'd been developed to have their own voice instead of being an expository set-piece, but as it stands, it's distracting and a waste of space.

The second issue is how far suspension-of-disbelief must be stretched to accommodate the notion that they will actually allow personnel to expose themselves apparently for shits and giggles. It reads to me as lolfoundation trying to be played straight, except it's nonsensical to begin with. I just can't conceive of the rationality behind it when they have guinea-pigs at their disposal. It's unpredictable, known to have a potentially detrimental effect on the subject's psyche, and has little actual value compared to conventional treatments.

This reminds me of SCP-948 "The Workaholic" by Rimple, but it's missing all the elements that makes it succeed. Where in 948, personnel exposure makes sense due to its predictability and beneficial nature - here it is haphazard, dangerous, and random. Where 948 hones in on its characters and delivers a concise story, here it reads like slapdash list of weird things, explanatory found-note interlude, and the beginning of a character study stapled to the end.

While the interviews provided some insight and raise important questions, it ends up being spoon-fed to the reader in an uninteresting fashion, by a poorly-established character with a set of contrived circumstances I simply cannot swallow. I would recommend strengthening the main character's personality, motives, and mind-set and try to have her develop over the course of the story - everything else is window dressing at the moment.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love the idea and the narrative really makes me want this to be a real person!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the good kind of bad, for me at least. She genuinely wants to help people, and she sort of kind of does. Reminds me of a Lord Dunsany short story about a man in Pegana who becomes one of the gods and tries to help humanity.

It goes about as well as this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't have done it without you, Zyn. Thank you so much for all the help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I'm glad that you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great to see this on the site at last! I've seen quite a few versions of the draft, and it's certainly come a long way development and quality-wise. The author's really put in a lot of hard work into this.

Good work, Othello! +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of SCP-040. Why can't we have more SCP's like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that the Foundation has a procedure in place for people to essentially sign up to become anomalies. I also like the "therapist who needs therapy" angle.

You could probably excise the last paragraph of the description, because it just repeats what's in the procedures. Otherwise, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh whoops! I completely forgot about those! Thanks for the help, and thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feexed the XXXX's for you. This version is better than the one I reviewed, so a +1.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to all the people who helped crit this:

Lovely people
Lovely faces

Thanks for the help from Soulless, Flawed, LadyKatie, Modern_Erasmus, Sly161, acc1177, Roget, Zero_Consistency, piceus, Weryllium, not_a_seagull, trots, ihp, and DrChandra (If I forgot you, please comment, and I will add you, because I have that sneaking suspicion that I might have forgotten someone)

And special thanks to Zyn for the bulk of the crits!

Also special thanks to my dad who read this article over despite knowing nothing about the SCP Foundation.

Image Source: https://pixabay.com/p-1180847/?no_redirect

I really hope you all enjoy!
